name: Korean

formality.description: Korean has seven speech levels (존댓말 jondaenmal = polite / 반말 banmal = casual). The three relevant to software are: 합쇼체 (hapsyo-che, highest formality — news anchors, military, government), 해요체 (haeyo-che, polite — standard for most software UI, natural and respectful), and 해체 (hae-che, casual — social media, gaming). Most Korean software uses 해요체.

polite-haeyo.label: 해요체 (Polite)
polite-haeyo.description: Standard for software UI, websites, and professional communication. Natural and respectful without being stiff.
polite-haeyo.prompt: Polite Korean (해요체). Standard register for software UI and professional communication. Natural and respectful without being stiff or overly deferential. Sentence endings use -요 forms. This is what users expect from apps, websites, and most digital products.

formal-hapsyo.label: 합쇼체 (Formal)
formal-hapsyo.description: For government, legal, corporate reports, or official announcements. Very formal.
formal-hapsyo.prompt: Formal Korean (합쇼체). Highest politeness level with -ㅂ니다/-습니다 endings. For government portals, legal text, corporate reports, and official announcements. This register is intentionally stiff and authoritative.

casual-hae.label: 해체 (Casual)
casual-hae.description: For social media, chat apps, gaming, and youth-oriented products.
casual-hae.prompt: Casual Korean (해체/반말). Plain form without politeness markers. For social media, messaging apps, gaming, and products targeting younger demographics. Natural conversational tone.

notes: The previous default (합쇼체) was too formal for software UI. Changed to 해요체, which is the actual industry standard for Korean digital products.
